Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd go days without touching anything, then suddenly spend like 30 hours cramming when I realized something was due. I know that sounds bad, but it somehow worked for me. I'd download everything I needed and then study in my room with my notes spread everywhere, usually late at night when it was quiet.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were coming up. Some weeks I'd barely look at course materials, other weeks I'd be pulling all-nighters. I have a lot going on outside of school, so I couldn't really stick to a regular schedule even if I wanted to. Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me into action.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or the gamified task notifications that made me log in. Sometimes I'd get scared that I was falling behind and panic-check what I'd missed. The points and badges from the gamified stuff were kind of motivating in a weird way, like a small dopamine hit. But honestly, if nothing was immediately due, I probably wasn't logging in.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my routine. I'd use it in bursts when I absolutely had to, but it never became a habit. The gamified elements helped a little because I'd want to check my progress, but even that wasn't enough to make it consistent. I'm just not someone who logs into things regularly.
Q5: The reading materials and written content were most useful to me because that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and articles and read them on my own time, highlighting and taking notes. The gamified tasks were actually kind of helpful too because they broke things into smaller pieces that felt less overwhelming. Videos were okay but I preferred having text I could control.
Q6: I completely ignored the discussion forums. Like, zero posts from me. It just felt awkward and performative, and I didn't see the point when I could just read on my own. I also skipped a lot of the optional videos because they took too long. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, I probably didn't prioritize it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I'd be graded on. If I could see how it would help me on an exam or assignment, I'd do it. The gamified tasks felt worth it because they gave me immediate feedback and points, which was satisfying. But vague 'enrichment' activities? Those got pushed aside.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Required ones had consequences, so I'd make myself do them even when I didn't want to. Optional stuff just fell off my radar because I was already stressed about keeping up with the mandatory work. I know I probably missed out on some useful content, but I had to prioritize.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline with downloaded PDFs and my own handwritten notes. I'd print things out when I could afford to, or read on my phone. I also used some external YouTube videos and websites when the course materials weren't clicking for me. The LMS was just where I got the materials, not where I actually learned.
Q10: Pretty much all the time, honestly. I'd download a lecture's worth of readings, log out, and then spend hours going through them with a notebook. My exam scores show I was learning, but my LMS activity looks like I barely touched anything. I'd also re-read my notes multiple times before exams, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere.
Q11: All the time. I'd access something once, download it, and never open it on the LMS again. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could study without wifi or when the LMS was being slow. Once I had the file, I didn't need to go back online. It was just more practical for my situation.
Q12: There were definitely moments when I felt scared and overwhelmed, like I was too far behind to catch up. When stress hit medium-to-high levels, I'd freeze up and avoid the course entirely for a few days. Having limited resources made things harder too because I couldn't always afford to print materials or had to share devices. Those periods showed up as gaps in my engagement.
Q13: Definitely. When I had work or family stuff competing for my attention, the LMS was the first thing to get neglected. Fatigue was a big factor too—after a long day, I just couldn't bring myself to log in and watch a video. Confusion about what was actually required also made me procrastinate because I didn't know where to start.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd open materials and just stare at them without absorbing anything. I'd be too tired or distracted to actually engage, but I'd leave the page open thinking I'd get to it. Other times I'd skim through something quickly just to say I'd seen it, even though I knew I wasn't really learning. It was like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially discussion posts and optional readings. Even when I knew they were important, the fear of not doing them well enough made me avoid them. It's easier to not try than to try and fail, I guess. The bigger assignments I'd put off until the last possible moment, then cram intensely.
Q16: Deadlines were everything. They were basically the only reason I engaged with the LMS at all. I'd check the calendar, see something was due, and then frantically work backward from there. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted even more than I already did. They created structure I couldn't create for myself.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but sometimes in a scared way. Good feedback made me feel relieved and motivated to keep going. But if I was worried about getting negative feedback, I'd avoid checking altogether. The gamified task feedback was easier to handle because it felt less personal and more like a game score.
Q18: The course structure was pretty loose, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work at my own pace, bad because I needed more external structure to stay on track. When modules were clearly laid out with specific deadlines, I did better. When things were vague, I drifted and fell behind.
Q19: The gamified elements actually made engagement easier because they broke things into smaller, achievable goals. That helped with my tendency to feel overwhelmed. But the discussion requirements made engagement harder because I just couldn't bring myself to post publicly. The lack of clear guidance on what was truly essential versus optional also made things confusing.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious and keeps checking things without actually completing them. Or maybe they're confused about what to do and keep logging in hoping it'll make sense. It could also be someone who's engaging with the materials but struggling to produce finished work. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That's literally me. I barely showed up on the LMS but I got a distinction because I was studying hard offline. I downloaded everything, made detailed notes, and really understood the material. My exam and assignment scores prove I was learning. The LMS just didn't capture how I actually worked.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone logs in and what they click on, but they miss everything that happens offline. They can't measure understanding or effort spent outside the platform. They also can't tell if someone's genuinely engaged or just clicking through mindlessly. The numbers are just a tiny piece of the picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're thinking about the concepts even outside of study time, making connections, wanting to learn more. For me, engagement happened mostly in my head and in my notes, not in my login frequency. It's internal, not performative.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent with downloaded materials, or how many pages of handwritten notes I made. It wouldn't show the fear I felt about falling behind or the relief when I finally understood something. It also wouldn't capture that despite looking disengaged online, I was actually deeply invested in doing well. My grades tell that story, but the logs don't.
